description: Spotlight profiles for key figures at the 1906 Alpine Club of Canada camp

key_figures.tagline: Visionary surveyor who founded the Alpine Club of Canada and orchestrated the first camp
key_figures.bio_summary: President of the ACC, organized the first camp. Surveyor by profession. Led scientific observations of Yoho Glacier. Chairman of Camp Committee.
key_figures.key_quote: Transparent emerald in Yoho lake; the Wapta Icefield, approximately twenty to twenty-five square miles, feeds four watersheds and holds the heart of the Canadian Rockies.

key_figures.tagline: Journalist whose vivid prose captured the wonder and adventure of the 1906 camp
key_figures.bio_summary: Writer and publicist. Authored the richest narrative account of the 1906 camp. His article is the primary source for camp life details.
key_figures.key_quote: At last, the summit of Yoho pass! At last, that striking picture of a tented town nestling amid the realm of trees... under a sky of blue, with grey old Wapta and Michael's mount standing sentinel.

key_figures.tagline: Fearless botanist who studied alpine flowers with uncompromising scientific rigor
key_figures.bio_summary: Botanist and author. Detailed the wildflowers of the Rockies and Selkirks. Notes Yellow Lady's Slipper (Cypripedium pubescens) growing on moraine from Emerald Lake to Yoho Valley in July.
key_figures.key_quote: Yellow Lady's Slippers were found on the moraine stretching from Emerald Lake to Yoho Valley in July, revealing the alpine botanical treasures of the Canadian Rockies.

key_figures.tagline: Clergyman-mountaineer who saw divinity in glaciers and peaks, captured on film
key_figures.bio_summary: Camp photographer. Made solo ascent of Mt. Stephen in October 1904 — described as remarkable. Assisted on multiple official climbs of Vice-President. Later famous for 1909 Mt. Robson attempt.
key_figures.key_quote: Mt. Stephen—the grim old King of the Rockies—stands guard above Field, holding within its heights the fossil beds and stone records of ancient seas.

key_figures.tagline: First conqueror of the Matterhorn, lending his legendary name to camp mountains
key_figures.bio_summary: Of Matterhorn fame. Named The Vice-President and The President. Visited Canadian Rockies 1901 with four Swiss guides. Honorary member ACC.
key_figures.key_quote: The Vice-President, so called by Mr. Edward Whymper of Matterhorn fame in honor of the CPR Vice-Presidency—a peak of considerable difficulty and danger.

key_figures.tagline: Master guide from Valais whose expertise made impossible peaks seem possible
key_figures.bio_summary: Swiss guide from Interlaken, loaned by CPR. Led climbs of Wapta, Collie. Accompanied Bridgland on Vice-President route scouting.
key_figures.key_quote: The Swiss guide Gottfried Feuz, leading expeditions up the demanding peaks, demonstrated the skill and courage that characterizes the mountain guides of Valais.

key_figures.tagline: Pioneering glaciologist whose camera documented vanishing ice, advancing climate science
key_figures.bio_summary: Photographer and glacier researcher. Part of the Vaux family of Philadelphia scientists. Authored guide to camping in the Rockies with detailed equipment lists.
key_figures.key_quote: No place more delightful than Lake O'Hara, a lovely clear sheet of water with glaciers from the slopes of Mt. Biddle breaking off in miniature icebergs.

key_figures.tagline: Visionary journalist and co-founder who shaped the Alpine Club of Canada
key_figures.bio_summary: Secretary of the ACC. Key founding figure. Address: 160 Furby Street, Winnipeg, Man. Wrote philosophical manifesto for the club.
key_figures.key_quote: The Alpine Club of Canada was born from a vision of bringing Canadians together to understand and appreciate the grandeur of their mountain heritage.

key_figures.tagline: Fearless mountaineer leading the most ambitious climbing expeditions from camp
key_figures.bio_summary: Outfitter from Glacier. Donated services for the camp. Climbed Mt. Wapta July 13.
key_figures.key_quote: Chief Mountaineer S.H. Baker of Glacier gave his services free to ensure the success of the first camp, embodying the spirit of Canadian mountaineering.
